coredata.dc:title: In silico study on graphene quantum dots modified with various functional groups inhibiting α‑synuclein dimerization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaoxiao
Wang, Gang
Zhao, Ziqian
Qian, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description:
Hypothesis: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with various functional groups are hypothesized to inhibit the α-synuclein (αS) dimerization, a crucial step in Parkinson’s disease pathogenesis. The potential of differently functionalized GQDs is systematically explored.
Experiments: All-atom replica-exchange molecular dynamics simulations (accumulating to 75.6 μs) in explicit water were performed to study the dimerization of the αS non-amyloid component region and the influence of GQDs modified with various functional groups. Conformation ensemble, binding behavior, and free energy analysis were conducted.
Findings: All studied GQDs inhibit β-sheet and backbone hydrogen bond formation in αS dimers, leading to looser oligomeric conformations. Charged GQDs severely impede the growth of extended β-sheets by providing extra contact surface. GQD binding primarily disrupts αS inter-peptide interactions through π-π stacking, CH-π interactions, and for charged GQDs, additionally through salt-bridge and hydrogen bonding interactions. GQD-COO– showed the most optimal inhibitory effect, binding mode, and intensity, which holds promise for the development of nanomedicines targeting amyloid aggregation in neurodegenerative diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Parkinson’s disease
α-synuclein aggregation
Graphene quantum dots
Inhibitory mechanism
Molecular dynamics simulation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQDs reduce β-sheet content, prevent backbone H-bond formation and shorten β-strand length of αS, with COO−-modified GQD being the most effective
GQDs reduce the formation of extended β-sheets, resulting in looser oligomeric conformations
GQDs inhibit αS dimerization mainly through hindering inter-peptide interactions, with relatively less effect on intra-peptide interaction pattern
Different GQDs exhibit distinct binding sites and intensity with αS, with GQD-COO− having the strongest binding ability and disrupting peptide-peptide interactions most significantly

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Computing Biomechanics Center of Shanghai University of Sport
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11932013, 11704256). Simulations were performed at the Computing Biomechanics Center of Shanghai University of Sport.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Influence of GQDs on the secondary structure of αS dimer. (a) Population of secondary structures for different systems. (b) β-sheet percentage of individual residue. (c) PDF of the number of backbone H-bonds. (d) Distribution of β-strand length. The simulated systems are distinguished by different colors, and the color code is consistent throughout the text.

Influence of GQDs on conformational ensembles of αS dimer. (a-f) Free-energy landscape (in kcal/mol) as a function of SASA and Rg of αS peptides. (g-l) Representative conformations of the first six most-populated clusters and corresponding proportions. The insets in the same row correspond to the same system.
The intra- and inter-peptide main chain-main chain contact probability maps for the αS dimer in the absence and presence of GQDs.

Binding sites and interactions of GQDs with αS. (a) Residue-based binding probability of GQDs. (b) Binding free energy between individual residue and GQDs. (c) Number of H-bonds formed by GQDs with each residue. (d) PDF of the contact area between αS and GQDs. (e,f) PDF of the minimum distance between GQDs and the benzene ring of F94 or the methyl group of I88. (g,h) Representative snapshots of the π-π interaction of F94 and the CH-π interaction of I88 with GQDs.
Schematic diagram for GQDs to prevent the formation of αS protofibrils by inhibiting αS dimerization and remodel the oligomeric conformations.

head.ce:title: In silico study on graphene quantum dots modified with various functional groups inhibiting α‑synuclein dimerization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Exercise and Health Sciences (Ministry of Education), Shanghai Frontiers Science Research Base of Exercise and Metabolic Health, and School of Exercise and Health, Shanghai University of Sport, 399 Changhai Road, Shanghai 200438, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Exercise and Health Sciences (Ministry of Education)
Shanghai Frontiers Science Research Base of Exercise and Metabolic Health, and School of Exercise and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Exercise and Health Sciences (Ministry of Education), Shanghai Frontiers Science Research Base of Exercise and Metabolic Health, and School of Exercise and Health, Shanghai University of Sport, 399 Changhai Road, Shanghai 200438, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypothesis: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with various functional groups are hypothesized to inhibit the α-synuclein (αS) dimerization, a crucial step in Parkinson’s disease pathogenesis. The potential of differently functionalized GQDs is systematically explored.

Experiments: All-atom replica-exchange molecular dynamics simulations (accumulating to 75.6 μs) in explicit water were performed to study the dimerization of the αS non-amyloid component region and the influence of GQDs modified with various functional groups. Conformation ensemble, binding behavior, and free energy analysis were conducted.

Findings: All studied GQDs inhibit β-sheet and backbone hydrogen bond formation in αS dimers, leading to looser oligomeric conformations. Charged GQDs severely impede the growth of extended β-sheets by providing extra contact surface. GQD binding primarily disrupts αS inter-peptide interactions through π-π stacking, CH-π interactions, and for charged GQDs, additionally through salt-bridge and hydrogen bonding interactions. GQD-COO– showed the most optimal inhibitory effect, binding mode, and intensity, which holds promise for the development of nanomedicines targeting amyloid aggregation in neurodegenerative diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common neurodegenerative disease after Alzheimer's disease [1], and there are approximately six million patients with PD worldwide [2]. The core clinical features of PD include resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability [3]. Several drugs such as levodopa, selegiline, amantadine and biperiden, can temporarily control the symptoms [4,5]; however, if the medication is stopped, the symptoms will deteriorate again or be even worse than before [6,7]. This highlights the importance of developing new drugs targeting more fundamental underlying pathogenic mechanisms of PD.

Accumulating evidence reveals that the PD pathogenesis is closely associated with the accumulation and propagation of aggregated α-synuclein (αS) in the midbrain [8,9]. αS is a 140-amino acid intrinsically disordered protein that is typically situated in neuronal pre-synaptic terminals [10]. Its N-terminal region (residues 1–60) has an amphipathic nature that facilitates membrane interactions [11]; the highly negatively charged C-terminal region (residues 96–140) is responsible for binding metal ions [12]; the central non-amyloid component (NAC) region (residues 61–95) under non-pathological conditions primarily participates in the axonal transport of α-synuclein within the axon [13]. The NAC region is the most aggregation-prone [14], harboring a hydrophobic segment of residues 71–82 that is indispensable for fibrillization [15,16].

Monomeric αS is unstructured under physiological conditions and forms α-helixes when binding to lipid membranes [17], while under PD pathological conditions, αS aggregates to form β-sheet structures leading to neuronal damage [18,19]. The fibrillization of αS exhibits common features of amyloid formation, proceeding through monomer misfolding, oligomerization, and rapid elongation via recruitment of additional peptides [20,21]. Among the various aggregates formed, small oligomeric intermediates are believed to be the most neurotoxic species [22,23]. The cell-to-cell propagation of αS pathology is facilitated by the release of seeding-competent oligomeric or fibrillar species from neurons, which can template the misfolding of monomeric αS, leading to the amplification and spread of aggregates [24,25]. Therefore, intervening in the early stage to prevent αS oligomerization is considered an effective strategy to inhibit αS aggregation.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an emerging functional material with excellent physiochemical properties, biocompatibility, and water dispersibility, which allows promising applications in drug delivery, bio-probing, imaging, and other nanotechnologies. GQDs have been demonstrated to inhibit the aggregation of amyloid proteins such as β-amyloid (Aβ) [26–28], human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) [29–31], αS [32,33], etc. Liu et al. found that GQDs carrying dissociated carboxyl groups efficiently inhibit Aβ aggregation and exhibit low cytotoxicity to PC-12 cells, and meanwhile have excellent photoluminescence properties and ability of crossing the blood–brain barrier [26]. Faridi et al. demonstrated that the exposure of pancreatic β-cells to monomeric, oligomeric, and fibrillar forms of hIAPP leads to significant disturbances in intracellular protein expression, and hydroxylated GQDs can effectively modulate the aberrant protein expression attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) and hydrophobic interaction with peptides [30]. The highly water dispersible fluorinated GQDs could not only alter the morphology of hIAPP aggregates, but also rescue the hIAPP-induced cytotoxicity of INS-1 cells [31]. Kim et al. discovered that GQDs carrying carboxyl groups can inhibit αS fibrillization, disaggregate mature fibrils, and protect against PD pathology and symptoms in cellular and animal models [32]. These in vitro, in vivo and in silico studies have greatly deepened our understanding of GQDs as amyloid inhibitors.

The amyloid inhibition of GQDs is impacted by several factors including their concentration, size, and surface modification. Studies have shown that increasing the concentration (up to 200 μg/mL) of GQDs carrying carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxide groups can enhance their inhibitory effect on the formation of αS fibrils [33], while the size of GQDs have varied impacts on amyloid aggregation, either inhibiting or accelerating it [34,35]. However, the molecular mechanism underlying the bioactivities of GQDs with αS remains elusive. This work systematically studies the effect of the functionalization of GQDs on their inhibitory ability. Using GQDs uniformly modified with various functional groups with different properties as templates, all-atom replica-exchange molecular dynamics (REMD) simulations (accumulating to 75.6 μs) in explicit water were performed to study the influence of GQDs on the dimerization of αS NAC region.

The fragment 61EQVTNVGGAV70VTGVTAVAQK80TVEGAGSIAA90ATGFV95 of αS NAC region was cropped from the crystal structure (PDB ID: 7NCG), with the N-terminus capped by CH3CO and the C-terminus capped by NH2. The highly homologous β-synuclein lacking the NAC region does not aggregate [36–38], indicating that the NAC region is essential for αS aggregation. The feasibility of targeting the NAC region to inhibit αS aggregation and reduce its cytotoxicity has also been proved [39,40]. Then the monomeric fragment was first subjected to a conventional MD simulation at 500 K to generate a coil-dominated conformation, which was used to construct the initial structures for REMD to accelerate convergence. For convenience, αS61−95 will be abbreviated as αS unless specified.

The GQD molecule here has a size of 10.8 × 11.2 Å2, referring to the previous works [41–43], and four identical functional groups were respectively modified at each corner of GQD (see Fig. S1), corresponding to different GQD models (i.e., GQD-CH3, GQD-OH, GQD-NH3 +, and GQD-COO−). Experimentally, GQDs modified with these functional groups have been successfully synthesized and characterized [42,44,45]. The topology and force field of GQDs were constructed as done in our previous study on GQD destabilizing hIAPP fibril [46].

Two αS monomers were then placed in the simulation box, with randomly oriented GQDs at a minimum inter-molecular distance of 1.5 nm from the monomers. The box was then solvated with TIP3P water [47] and ionized with Na+ and Cl− to neutralize the charge and obtain a NaCl concentration of 0.15 M. In accordance with experimental conditions, the molar ratio of GQD molecules to αS peptides was set to 1:1 [32]. Six systems were studied by REMD simulations: isolated αS61-95 dimer, labeled as αS system; αS in the presence of kinds of GQDs, labeled as αS + GQD, αS + GQD-CH3, αS + GQD-OH, αS + GQD-NH3 +, and αS + GQD-COO− systems. Each system includes 42 replicas, with temperatures ranging from 305 K to 385 K. The simulation duration for each replica is 300 ns. More details are given in Table S1.

REMD simulations were carried out utilizing the Gromacs-2018.4 software suite [48] under isothermal-isobaric (NPT) conditions. The AMBER99SB-ILDN force field [49] which enhances side chain torsional potentials, was implemented. Previous benchmarking studies [50–52] have demonstrated the suitability and robustness of this force field for modeling the conformational landscapes of intrinsically disordered proteins like αS, capturing their inherent flexibility and disorder. All bonds were constrained by the LINCS algorithm [53] and the integration time step was 2 fs. Periodic boundary conditions were imposed along all three axes. Coupling at 1 bar was attained by the Parrinello-Rahman barostat [54], with a time constant of 1.0 ps, while temperature coupling employed velocity rescaling [55] with a time constant of 0.2 ps. Electrostatics were handled by the particle-mesh Ewald technique [56] with a real-space cutoff of 1.0 nm, and van der Waals interactions were determined with a cutoff of 1.0 nm.

Trajectory analysis was performed using in-house developed codes and utilities in the Gromacs-2018.4 software package. Secondary structures of peptides were calculated using the DSSP program [57], and sample conformations were classified by Daura clustering [58] with a Cα-root-mean-square deviation (Cα-RMSD) cutoff of 0.35 nm. An atomic contact was defined when two non-hydrogen atoms came within 0.54 nm. Hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) were identified with a donor–acceptor distance less than 0.35 nm and a donor-hydrogen-acceptor angle larger than 150°. Salt bridges between E61 / E83 and K80 side chains were considered formed when the minimal distance between nitrogen atoms of the K80 / K83 NH3 + group and oxygen atoms of the E61 COO– group was within 0.40 nm. The free energy landscapes were computed using the formula –RTlnH(x,y), where H(x,y) is the histogram of two selected reaction coordinates: solvent accessible surface area (SASA) and radius of gyration (Rg) of αS peptides. SASA was calculated with a water probe radius of 0.14 nm. Binding free energies were estimated by the molecular mechanics/generalized Born surface area (MM/GBSA) method [59,60]. The GB-OBC2 model and the mbondi2 radius set [61,62] were used here. This radius set was optimized together with the atomic charges of the AMBER99SB force field, and using the matching atomic radii and charge parameters ensures the accuracy and consistency of the model.

Before data analysis, the convergence of the six REMD simulation series were validated over two independent time periods by contrasting multiple metrics at 310 K. Figs. S2-S6 show that these metrics, including the distribution of secondary structures, the probability density function (PDF) of SASA, PDF of H-bond number, PDF of Rg, and PDF of Cα-RMSD, indicate that the final 100 ns data from the six systems have properly converged. Thus, our analyses are based on the last 100 ns data at 310 K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs reduce β-sheet content, prevent backbone H-bond formation and shorten β-strand length of αS, with COO−-modified GQD being the most effective
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of GQDs on the secondary structure of αS dimerization, Fig. 1 a presents the proportions of different secondary structures. For easy comparison, the GQDs with modifications of hydrophobic/hydrophilic or charged group are divided into two groups and compared with isolated αS and αS + GQD systems, respectively. Upon introducing GQDs, the β-sheet content substantially decreases; the coil, bend, turn and helix proportions elevate, whereas the β-bridge remains almost unchanged. The observed GQD-induced β-sheet reduction is consistent with previous experimental results using thioflavin T fluorescence and circular dichroism spectra [32,33]. Among all modifications, hydroxylated and carboxylated GQDs lead to the largest decreases in β-sheet content and increases in coil content. GQD-OH reduces the β-sheet content from 21.4 % to 13.8 %, while GQD-COO− decreases it to 10.0 %. To further identify the amino acid that is most influenced, the β-sheet propensity of individual residues is assessed in Fig. 1b. It can be seen that isolated αS has four regions with high β-sheet propensity, corresponding to four distribution peaks. These β-sheet regions encompass the high aggregation-prone fragments located in the NAC region obtained from previous MD and NMR studies [63,64]. The β-sheet percentages of almost all the residues decrease after the addition of GQDs. Interestingly, hydrophobic/hydrophilic group-modified GQDs do not change the residue-based β-sheet distribution; positively charged group-modified GQDs eliminate the second β-sheet peak, while negatively charged ones removed both the second and third peaks. Given the high aggregation propensity of the αS71-82 region and the fact that removing this region prevents full-length αS from fibrillization [15,16], decreasing the second and third β-sheet distribution peaks contained in this region would greatly weaken the aggregation ability of αS. These results indicate that the various GQDs studied here all reduce β-sheet formation in αS dimers, with negatively charged modification having the greatest reduction.

The impact of GQDs on H-bond formation is inspected by evaluating the PDF of backbone H-bond number in Fig. 1c. The addition of GQDs leads to a decrease in the number of backbone H-bonds, suggesting that GQDs suppress H-bond formation. Hydrophilic modification leads to greater reductions in H-bonds than hydrophobic modification, while negatively charged modification results in the largest decrease in H-bond numbers among all systems. Previous studies reported that the hydroxylated GQD could largely cleave off the H-bonds formed between the main chains of hIAPP through H-bonding and hydrophobic interactions [30]. Here, it is observed that hydrophilic GQDs can hinder the oligomerization of αS through the same mechanism. Distribution of β-strand length in Fig. 1d shows the influence of GQDs on the number of consecutive residues adopting β-sheet conformation. Note that due to the different β-sheet contents of different systems, only the distribution within each system should be referred to. As for isolated αS, αS + GQD, αS + GQD-CH3 and αS + GQD-OH systems, β-strand lengths longer than six are relatively rare; as for αS + GQD-NH3 + system, β-strand lengths of four or less make up the vast majority; as for αS + GQD-COO− system, β-strand lengths of two and three predominate. It indicates that charged GQDs severely inhibit the growth of longer β-sheets that are important for the formation of toxic oligomers and αS fibrillogenesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs reduce the formation of extended β-sheets, resulting in looser oligomeric conformations
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of GQDs on the αS conformational ensembles was further probed by analyzing the two-dimensional free energy landscape as a function of two reaction coordinates SASA and Rg of the αS dimer across the six systems (Fig. 2 a-f). There is only one major free energy basin in isolated αS system, situated at (SASA = 45.8 nm2, Rg = 1.16 nm). In the presence of GQD / GQD-CH3 / GQD-OH, there remains one primary energy basin, but its center corresponds to larger SASA and Rg values, reflecting that these GQDs expose more residues to the aqueous environment and make αS conformations less compact. In the presence of GQD-NH3 + / GQD-COO−, several minor energy basins emerge, and the overall energy landscape is greatly expanded, reflecting that these GQDs not only make the αS dimer looser, but also greatly increase the structural diversity.

To identify representative conformations of the αS dimer, the sampled oligomer structures were additionally clustered utilizing the Daura approach, resulting in 210, 241, 261, 228, 221 and 350 clusters for αS, αS + GQD, αS + GQD-CH3, αS + GQD-OH, αS + GQD-NH3 + and αS + GQD-COO− systems, respectively. The representative conformations of the first six most-populated clusters and corresponding proportions were presented in Fig. 2g-l. The snapshots show that isolated αS forms conformations enriched in β-sheets. Together with the β-strand length results, these oligomers containing extended β-sheets are important for the structural transformation into fibrillar aggregates. These intra- and inter-peptide extended β-sheets have also been observed in previous computational studies on αS and are identified as crucial intermediates for aggregation [65–67]. Experimental evidence also indicates that toxic αS oligomers possess β-sheet or β-hairpin structures [68,69]. With addition of GQDs, the αS peptides are separated by the GQDs, which is unfavorable for peptide-peptide H-bonding and thus produces more coils. Interestingly, GQDs with charge modifications result in αS forming shorter β-sheets. Previous thioflavin T fluorescence studies have shown that negatively charged GQDs exhibit better inhibition effects against Aβ aggregation compared to uncharged ones, and the more negative charges the GQDs carry, the better their inhibition effects on Aβ [26]. Our results show that the two GQDs without charge modifications tend to stick together due to strong π-π stacking and allow fewer binding sites for αS, while charged GQDs electrostatically repel each other and provide more surface area to interact with αS. Figs. S7 and S8 show that the distance between charged GQDs is much larger than that between GQDs with other functionalization. The GQD-GQD distance analysis in αS + GQD-COO- system (Fig. S9) shows that GQDs adopting non-self-aggregating state account for 15.24 % of the total. These reveal that GQDs can inhibit the formation of extended β-sheets and lead to looser conformations of αS dimer. In addition, the charged GQDs can more effectively separate the peptides, and therefore the corresponding αS conformations have shorter β-strands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs inhibit αS dimerization mainly through hindering inter-peptide interactions, with relatively less effect on intra-peptide interaction pattern
ce:section.ce:para: The key residues involved in αS interactions and the influence of GQDs were probed in Figs. 3 and S10 by calculating the contact probability map between residue pairs to estimate intra- and inter-peptide interactions between main chains (MC-MC) or side chains (SC-SC). In the isolated αS system, the MC interaction pattern shows a strong intra-peptide interaction between regions 75TAVAQKTV82 and 85AGSIAAA91. Accompanied with GQDs, the intra-peptide interactions are weakened to different extents, but the overall interaction patterns are retained. The inter-peptide MC interactions are extensively hindered by GQDs, with GQD-COO− exhibiting the most significant weakening effect. The situation of SC-SC interactions affected by GQDs is similar. GQDs primarily weaken the inter-peptide SC interactions and GQD-COO− displays the most pronounced effect. These findings indicate that GQDs are able to effectively disrupt the inter-peptide interactions of αS, and the altered interaction features within the oligomers induced by GQDs are expected to hamper αS aggregation and cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different GQDs exhibit distinct binding sites and intensity with αS, with GQD-COO− having the strongest binding ability and disrupting peptide-peptide interactions most significantly
ce:section.ce:para: The binding sites and interactions of GQDs with αS are presented in Fig. 4 . The binding probability and free energy between individual residue and GQDs show that all kinds of GQDs preferentially bind to the aromatic residue F94, followed by I88, indicating the critical roles of π-π stacking and CH-π interactions. For GQD-NH3 + or GQD-COO−, they also have a high affinity of binding to E83 or K80, indicating the salt-bridge interaction plays an important role in the binding of charged GQDs. The residue-based H-bond number shows that GQD-COO− forms the most H-bonds with αS, with residues K80, T92 and T81 contributing the most; GQD-NH3 + forms multiple H-bonds with αS, primarily involving E83 and E61; GQD-OH formed the fewest H-bonds with αS. The PDF of the contact area between αS and GQDs shows that charged GQDs have a significantly increased contact area than other GQDs, with GQD-COO− having slightly higher contact area than GQD-NH3 +. This can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsion remarkably reducing contacts between the two GQDs, exposing more surface for αS adsorption. The distribution of minimum distance between GQDs and F94 or I88 was calculated to estimate π-π stacking or CH-π interaction. For π-π stacking interaction, the PDF of GQD-NH3 + has the highest peak, followed by that of GQD / GQD-COO−; for CH-π interactions, GQD / GQD-COO− peaks are tied for the highest. As π-π stacking and CH-π interactions are significant in ligand binding and molecular recognition [70,71], the analysis of GQD binding to F94 or I88 (Figs. S11, S12) indicates their long-lasting interaction, reflecting that the π-π stacking and CH-π interactions are potent and prevalent. Taken together, it can be concluded that the binding behavior of GQDs is largely dominated by π-π stacking and CH-π interactions; as for charged GQDs, the salt-bridge and H-bonding interactions are synergistically involved to facilitate the binding.

The binding free energy of GQDs with αS was calculated by MM/GBSA method in Table S2. The value of ΔGbind between one peptide and GQDs without modifications is −41.54 kcal/mol; after functionalization, the binding energy of GQDs gets lower corresponding to a stronger binding intensity, with GQD-COO− exhibiting the lowest energy of −49.13 kcal/mol. As for αS + GQD-COO− system, ΔGsol (ΔGpol + ΔGnonpol) is quite negative, reflecting the solvation effect greatly strengthens the binding of negatively charged GQDs with αS. Table S3 shows the binding free energy between the two peptides of αS dimer. In the absence of GQDs, the value is −77.18 kcal/mol; introduced with GQD, GQD-CH3, GQD-OH, GQD-NH3 + and GQD-COO−, the value changes to −40.72, −43.75, −34.54, −29.43 and –22.52 kcal/mol. These results indicate that peptide-GQD interaction greatly weakens the peptide-peptide interaction, thereby hindering peptide associations and remodeling the oligomeric structures, which goes against subsequent αS aggregation.

The proposed inhibitory mechanisms are summarized in Fig. 5 . At neutral pH, the NAC region contains negatively charged E61, E83 and positively charged K80, while the full-length αS has more positively charged Lys distributed in the N-terminal region, while negatively charged Asp and Glu are mainly distributed in the C-terminal region. This may lead to different conclusions regarding the optimal GQD modification. Still, the modification of charged group is probably the most promising choice, for charged GQDs provide more exposed surface for binding sites due to electrostatic repulsion. As the NAC region is critical in stabilizing αS fibrils [72] and inhibitors against this region reduces intracellular aggregation and toxicity [39,40], targeting the NAC region is important for amyloid inhibition [73]. This work primarily focuses on simulation computing and mechanistic interpretation. We hope that this study will inspire other experimental teams to verify the ability of functionalized GQDs to inhibit amyloid formation.

In this work, all-atom REMD simulations (with a total time of 75.6 μs) in explicit water were performed to systematically study the influence of differently functionalized GQDs on the dimerization of αS NAC region. The various GQDs studied here can inhibit the formation of β-sheets and backbone H-bonds in αS dimers, leading to looser oligomeric conformations. The charged GQDs severely impede the growth of extended β-sheets that are critical for αS fibril formation and cytotoxicity, by providing extra contact surface. The inhibitory effect of GQDs is mainly attributed to the disruption of inter-peptide interactions, with relatively less effect on intra-peptide interaction pattern. All types of GQDs preferentially bind to F94 and I88, indicating the binding behavior of GQDs is governed by π-π stacking and CH-π interactions. For charged GQDs, salt-bridge and H-bonding interactions also synergistically participate to enhance binding affinity. The free energy calculation shows that the binding of GQDs to αS greatly weakens the peptide-peptide interaction, which would remodel the oligomeric structures and hinder further αS aggregation. In addition, GQD-COO− is suggested to be the most promising candidate of amyloid inhibition, given its superior inhibitory effect, binding mode, and binding intensity among the studied types of GQDs. Our findings reveal the molecular mechanisms of various functionalizations modulating the inhibitory effects of GQDs, which will facilitate the development of anti-amyloid nanomedicines and early diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases based on amyloid peptides.

Xiaoxiao Wu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Gang Wang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ziqian Zhao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhenyu Qian: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11932013, 11704256). Simulations were performed at the Computing Biomechanics Center of Shanghai University of Sport.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.04.111.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Challenges in the diagnosis of Parkinson's disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Tolosa, A. Garrido, S.W. Scholz, W. Poewe, Challenges in the diagnosis of Parkinson's disease, Lancet Neurol. 20 (5) (2021) 385-397, DOI: 10.1016/s1474-4422(21)00030-2.
B. Zhu, D. Yin, H. Zhao, L. Zhang, The immunology of Parkinson’s disease, Semin. Immunopathol. 44 (5) (2022) 659-672, DOI: 10.1007/s00281-022-00947-3.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nishio, Y. Umezawa, M. Hirota, Y. Takeuchi, The CH/π interaction: significance in molecular recognition, Tetrahedron 51 (32) (1995) 8665-8701, DOI: 10.1016/0040-4020(94)01066-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A systematic analysis of atomic protein–ligand interactions in the PDB

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.F. de Freitas, M. Schapira, A systematic analysis of atomic protein–ligand interactions in the PDB, Medchemcomm 8 (10) (2017) 1970-1981, DOI: 10.1039/C7MD00381A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupling of the non-amyloid-component (NAC) domain and the KTK (E/Q) GV repeats stabilize the α-synuclein fibrils

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xu, R. Nussinov, B. Ma, Coupling of the non-amyloid-component (NAC) domain and the KTK (E/Q) GV repeats stabilize the α-synuclein fibrils, Eur. J. Med. Chem. 121 (2016) 841-850, DOI: 10.1016/j.ejmech.2016.01.044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging disease-modifying strategies targeting α-synuclein for the treatment of Parkinson's disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. O'Hara, S.K. Kalia, L.V. Kalia, Emerging disease‐modifying strategies targeting α‐synuclein for the treatment of Parkinson's disease, Brit. J. Pharmacol. 175 (15) (2018) 3080-3089, DOI: 10.1111/bph.14345.
